coredata.dc:title: A novel and sensitive fluorescent assay for artemisinin with graphene quantum dots based on inner filter effect
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Shuyun
Yan, Xiaolu
Sun, Jing
Zhao, Xian-En
Wang, Xiao
coredata.dc:description: The authors describe a novel method for the determination of artemisinin (ART) by using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as the fluorescent probes. This method is based on the fact that ART can react with p-aminophenylboronic acid (p-ABA) to produce p-aminophenol (p-AP). While in the presence of tyrosinase (TYR), p-AP can be oxidized into 4-amino-1,2-benzoquinone, which effectively quenched the fluorescence of GQDs due to the inner filter effect (IFE). By making use of these reactions, a novel and sensitive fluorescent assay for ART has been developed. The calibration curve for the determination of ART is linear in the range of 0.1–5 μM and 5–55 μM with the detection limit of 33 nM, which is more sensitive than most of other methods. Some common coexisting substances including Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, K+, PO4 3-, starch, lactose, dextrin, and magnesium stearat have negligible effects on the fluorescence intensity of GQDs-TYR-p-ABA system. Finally, the sensing system was successfully applied to the detection of the compound naphthoquine phosphate tablet samples with satisfactory recoveries. This IFE-based GQDs fluorescence sensing strategy is facile and sensitive for the determination of ART because neither the surface modification nor the linking between the receptor and the fluorophore is required.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Artemisinin
Graphene quantum dots
Fluorescent assay
Inner filter effect
Pharmaceutical analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedure for the determination of ART
Procedure for analysis of content of ART in the tablet
Assessment of detection strategy for ART based on GQDs and possible mechanism

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The low-magnification and (B) the high-magnification TEM images of GQDs; (C) UV–vis absorption spectrum of GQDs, excitation spectrum of GQDs at λem = 457 nm, and emission spectrum of GQDs at λex = 375 nm; (D) Fluorescence spectra of GQDs under different excitation wavelength indicated.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs (a), GQDs + p-AP (b), GQDs + TYR (c), GQDs + p-AP + TYR (d), and GQDs + p-ABA + ART + TYR (e). (B) Fluorescence intensity decay curves of GQDs in the absence (a) and presence (b) of p-ABA, ART, and TYR. [GQDs] = 3.5 mg/mL; [p-AP] = 45 μM; [TYR] = 50 μg/mL; [p-ABA] = 0.25 mM; [ART] = 55 μM.

The effects of reaction time (A) (curve a: enzyme reaction time; curve b: the reaction time between 4-amino-1,2-benzoquinone and GQDs), pH (B), temperature (C), and concentration of TYR (D) on the detection of p-AP. The other conditions are the same as Fig. 2.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs in the presence of various concentrations of ART; (B) Plot of the quenching efficiency (F 0-F)/F 0 versus the concentrations of ART introduced. The other conditions are the same as Fig. 2.

Fluorescence responses of GQDs towards various analytes. Error bars showed the standard deviations from three independent experiments. From 1–10 was ART, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, K+, PO4 3-, starch, lactose, dextrin, and magnesium stearate, respectively. The concentration of each analyte was 50 μM.
The schematic illustration of the principle procedure for the determination of ART by using GQDs-based fluorescent nanosensor.
Comparison of different methods for the determination of ART.
Determination of ART in compound naphthoquine phosphate tablets.

head.ce:title: A novel and sensitive fluorescent assay for artemisinin with graphene quantum dots based on inner filter effect

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qufu Normal University, Qufu City, Shandong Province 273165, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qufu Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Qinghai Key Laboratory of Qinghai-Tibet Plateau Biological Resources, Northwest Institute of Plateau Biology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Xining City, Qinghai Province 810001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Qinghai Key Laboratory of Qinghai-Tibet Plateau Biological Resources, Northwest Institute of Plateau Biology, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Key Laboratory of TCM Quality Control Technology, Shandong Analysis and Test Center, Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), 19 Keyuan Street, Jinan 250014, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Key Laboratory of TCM Quality Control Technology, Shandong Analysis and Test Center, Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The authors describe a novel method for the determination of artemisinin (ART) by using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as the fluorescent probes. This method is based on the fact that ART can react with p-aminophenylboronic acid (p-ABA) to produce p-aminophenol (p-AP). While in the presence of tyrosinase (TYR), p-AP can be oxidized into 4-amino-1,2-benzoquinone, which effectively quenched the fluorescence of GQDs due to the inner filter effect (IFE). By making use of these reactions, a novel and sensitive fluorescent assay for ART has been developed. The calibration curve for the determination of ART is linear in the range of 0.1–5 μM and 5–55 μM with the detection limit of 33 nM, which is more sensitive than most of other methods. Some common coexisting substances including Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, K+, PO4 3-, starch, lactose, dextrin, and magnesium stearat have negligible effects on the fluorescence intensity of GQDs-TYR-p-ABA system. Finally, the sensing system was successfully applied to the detection of the compound naphthoquine phosphate tablet samples with satisfactory recoveries. This IFE-based GQDs fluorescence sensing strategy is facile and sensitive for the determination of ART because neither the surface modification nor the linking between the receptor and the fluorophore is required.

An inner filter effect-based fluorescent assay has been developed for the determination of artemisinin by employing graphene quantum dots as the probes.
fx1

ce:section.ce:para: Malaria is a mosquito-borne infectious disease and continues to be a major health problem in the tropical and subtropical countries [1]. In 2016, about 216 million cases of malaria and an estimated 445,000 deaths took place worldwide. Artemisinin (ART), isolated from Chinese medicinal plant Artemisia annua, possess rapid action against plasmodium falciparum malaria [2–4]. Ascribed to its standard treatment of malarial, its discoverer, Youyou Tu, was awarded half of the 2015 Noble Prize in medicine. At present, ART and its derivatives have received increasing attention due to their anthelmintic, antiviral, antifungal, and anticancer activity [5,6]. However, the substandard antimalarial medications and supplements with inadequate or too much active pharmaceutical ingredient are a threat to the peoples’ health. Thus, there is an increasing interest in the development of a simple, selective and sensitive analytical method for the determination of ART. The determination of ART in pharmaceuticals can provide a basis for drug quality control. Meanwhile, the determination of ART in biological fluids such as plasma and serum can provide data support for pharmacokinetic and bioequivalence studies of drugs containing ART.

To date, several methods have been developed for the determination of ART, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [6], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [7], chemiluminescence [8], molecular imprinting sensor [9], electrochemistry [10–12] and fluorescence [13]. Compared with other methods, the fluorescent analysis route has attracted great attentions because of its high sensitivity, high accuracy, and essential simplicity [14,15]. However, current fluorescent methods for measuring ART need organic fluorescent dyes, which have poor photostability and solubility in water [13].

With the development of nanoscience and nanotechnology, the fluorescent nanomaterials have emerged with some distinguished advantages in the design of various fluorescent sensors [16–18]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a new kind of zero-dimensional carbonaceous nanomaterials [19–22]. Due to their unique properties including low cytotoxicity, chemical stability and photostability, biocompatibility, and low cost, fluorescent sensors based on GQDs have been developed to detect various analytes, such as metal ions [23], biological small molecules [24–26], DNA [27], enzymes [28–30], antigen [31], and so on. However, the fluorescent detection of ART based on GQDs has not been reported up to date.

Herein, we developed a novel and sensitive fluorescent method for the detection of ART by employing GQDs as the fluorescent probes. The pristine GQDs displayed an emission peak at 457 nm upon excitation at 375 nm. It has been reported that ART could selective oxidize p-aminophenylboronic acid (p-ABA) to generate p-aminophenol (p-AP) [10]. Tyrosinase (TYR), as a copper-containing monooxygenase enzyme, can catalyse the hydroxylation of phenolic substrates to catechol derivatives, which can be further oxidized to benzoquinone [32]. Thus, in the presence of TYR, p-AP was oxidized into 4-amino-1,2-benzoquinone, which effectively quenched the fluorescence of GQDs due to the inner filter effect (IFE) ( Scheme 1). This IFE-based sensor does not require the surface modification of GQDs or establishing of any covalent between the acceptor and the fluorophore, providing considerable flexibility and simplicity in the probe fabrication. Therefore, this approach provides a novel, sensitive, and highly desirable method for the determination of ART.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were obtained from XFNANO (Nanjing, China, www.xfnano.com). p-Aminophenylboronic acid (p-ABA) was obtained from Apollo Scientific Ltd. (USA, www.apolloscientific.co.uk). The tyrosinase (TYR) from mushroom were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (USA, www.sigma-aldrich.com). Artemisinin (ART), p-aminophenol (p-AP), starch, lactose, dextrin, magnesium stearate, and metal salts (KNO3, CaCl2, Na3PO4, and MgCl2) were purchased from Aladdin Company (Shanghai, China, www.aladdin-e.com). The compound naphthoquine phosphate tablets were brought from Kunming Pharmaceutical Company (Kunming, China, www.kpc.com.cn). Phosphate buffer of various pH were prepared with different ratios of Na2HPO4 and NaH2PO4. The stock solution of both ART and p-ABA were prepared in ethanol. All reagents were of analytical reagent grade and used as received. Doubly deionized water was used throughout.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained using a Philips CM200 FEG microscope. All fluorescence measurements were carried out on a fluorescence spectropohotometer (Max-4, HORIBA, USA) operated at an excitation wavelength at 375 nm, with both excitation and emission slit widths of 5.0 nm. The fluorescence intensities were collected at 457 nm. Fluorescence lifetime measurements were carried out in the time-correlated single-photon counting unit of a Horiba-Jobin-Yvon Fluorolog-3 spectrofluorometer (NJ, USA). The UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV-3600, Japan). The desired pH buffer solutions were adjusted with pHS-3C pH meter (Shanghai, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for the determination of ART
ce:section.ce:para: The detection procedures included reaction step and detection step. Briefly, 10 μL of appropriate concentrations of ART in ethanol was mixed with 9 μL of 10 mM p-ABA in ethanol and 25 μL of 50 mM NaOH ethanol/water (V/V: 9/1) solution. The above mixture reacted for 10 min. Then, 10 μL of 1 mg/mL TYR and 141 μL of pH = 7.0 PBS buffer solution were added into the resulting solutions. After incubation at 40 °C for 10 min, 5 μL of 20 mg/mL GQDs solution was added into the above mixture and reacted at room temperature for 10 min. Finally, the fluorescence spectra were measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for analysis of content of ART in the tablet
ce:section.ce:para: A pill of compound naphthoquine phosphate tablet was thoroughly grinded using agate mortar. The resulting powder was added into 5 mL ethanol. The dispersion was stirred for three minutes, and then precipitate naturally. The clear supernatant fraction was collected for fluorescent detection.

TEM was used to examine the morphology and size of GQDs. As shown in Fig. 1A, GQDs were uniform with the average size of 2.4–4.0 nm in diameter. In addition, the TEM image revealed that GQDs were well-dispersed in water and well-separated from each other, as evidenced by the high-magnification image (Fig. 1B).

The optical characteristics of GQDs were systematically characterized by employing UV–vis spectroscopy and fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 1C, GQDs exhibited the broad absorption band with a typical absorption peak at around 330 nm, which was ascribed to the π-π* electronic transition in C˭C sp2 carbon domains of carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials resulting in the strong fluorescence of GQDs [33]. The fluorescence emissions of GQDs with different excitation wavelengths were also investigated. Like most of the fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials, GQDs also exhibited an excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior, presumably resulting from the different surface state emission trap of these carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials [34,35]. As shown in Fig. 1D, with the excitation wavelengths increasing from 340 nm to 410 nm, the maximum fluorescence peaks of GQDs shifted gradually to the longer wavelength direction. Moreover, the fluorescence peak intensities of GQDs firstly increased and then decreased subsequently. The maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of GQDs were observed at 375 nm and 457 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of detection strategy for ART based on GQDs and possible mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the feasibility of GQDs for the detection of ART was assessed. The fluorescent emission spectra of GQDs under different conditions were recorded. As shown in Fig. 2, GQDs had a 457 nm emission peak (curve a), which was not influenced by p-AP (curve b) or TYR (curve c), respectively. When both TYR and p-AP existed, the fluorescence of GQDs at 457 nm quenched efficiently (curve d). According to the previous report, ART could oxidize p-ABA to generate p-AP [11]. The p-AP was oxidized by TYR into 4-amino-1,2-benzoquinone, which caused the fluorescence quenching of GQDs (curve e). Thus, the effective quenching of the fluorescence of GQDs by ART, p-ABA, and TYR can be used for the quantitative detection of ART.

Then, we investigated the possible detection mechanism. As shown in Fig. S1A, TYR, ART or p-ABA had no obvious UV–vis absorption in the range of 350–700 nm. However, after the incubation of TYR with the mixture of ART and p-ABA for 10 min, the mixed solution had an obvious absorption peak at 400 nm, which indicated the generation of 4-amino-1,2-benzoquinone. Since there was the spectral overlap between the excitation/emission spectrum and the absorption spectrum of 4-amino-1,2-benzoquinone (Fig. S1B), the inner filter effect (IFE) or the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) based fluorescence quenching might occur.

The zeta potentials of GQDs and 4-amino-1,2-benzoquinone were measured. The GQDs exhibited the zeta potential of −9.5 mV while the zeta potential of 4-amino-1,2-benzoquinone was measured as −21.15 mV (data not shown). It was deduced that it was not possible that electrostatic interaction between the negatively charged GQDs and 4-amino-1,2-benzoquinone resulted in the fluorescence quenching, excluding the possibility of FRET mechanism [36].

On the other hand, both FRET and photoinduced electron transfer (PET) belong to the dynamic quenching mechanism because such energy/electron transfer just occurs in the excited-state and is expected to substantially change the lifetime of the fluorophore [37,38]. To further reveal the mechanism of fluorescence quenching, time-resolved fluorescence spectra of GQDs in the absence and presence of ART, p-ABA, and TYR were measured. As shown in Fig. 2B, GQDs showed a single lifetime of 4.27 ns (curve a), which agreed with the previously reported value [39]. Meanwhile, the presence of ART, p-ABA, and TYR had negligible influences on the fluorescence lifetime of the GQDs (curve b). Thus, the dynamic quenching could be excluded in this sensing system. From the above results, we can safely conclude that the sensing strategy was based on IFE. With the aid of this principle, a novel and convenient approach for ART detection can be developed just by simply mixing fluorophore and quencher.

To achieve the best analytical performance for ART, several parameters involved in the detection of p-AP were firstly optimized, including incubation time, pH, temperature, and the concentration of TYR. The fluorescence quenching efficiency (F 0-F)/F 0 was used as a standard for choosing the best condition, where F 0 and F referred the fluorescence intensities of GQDs in the absence and presence of p-AP and TYR, respectively.

The enzyme reaction time between p-AP and TYR is an important factor for the detection system since it is directly related with the amounts of 4-amino-1,2-benzoquinone. This enzyme reaction time was investigated from 1 min to 20 min (curve a, Fig. 3A). It can be seen that the (F 0-F)/F 0 increased firstly with the increasing time from 1 min to 10 min and then stayed unchanged when the time was more than 10 min. As a result, 10 min was chosen as the optimal enzyme reaction time. Second, the effects of reaction time between the generated 4-amino-1,2-benzoquinone and GQDs were also studied (curve b, Fig. 3A). The results indicated that the (F 0-F)/F 0 became constant during 30 min of reaction time. Thus, 30 min was chosen as the optimal incubation time between GQDs and 4-amino-1,2-benzoquinone. Third, the influences of pH values were evaluated using different pH values. As shown in Fig. 3B, the (F 0-F)/F 0 changed slightly in the pH range of 4.0–9.0, indicating that p-AP could be detected within such a wide pH range. Since the pH of biological matrixes is about 7.0, a pH of 7.0 was used in the following experiments. The enzyme reaction temperature was another important factor for the detection system. The enzyme reaction temperature was investigated ranging from 20 to 50 °C. As show in Fig. 3C, the best (F 0-F)/F 0 was obtained at 40 °C, indicating TYR exhibited the maximum activity at 40 °C. Thus, 40 °C was chosen as the optimum enzyme reaction temperature. Finally, the effect of concentration of TYR was investigated. As described in Fig. 3D, the (F 0-F)/F 0 enhanced with the increasing concentration of TYR until it reached to 50 μg/mL, which was chosen as the optimal concentrations of TYR for further investigations.

Under the optimal conditions, we investigated the responses of this system towards p-AP. As shown in Fig. S2, with the increasing concentration of p-AP, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs decreased gradually. Meanwhile, along with the p-AP concentrations increasing from 0.5 to 50 μM, the fluorescence intensity values at 457 nm displayed a quasi-linear relationship. These results indicated the feasibility of this method for the detection of p-AP.

According to the previous report, p-ABA could selective oxidize ART to generate p-AP [10]. Thus, GQDs could be used to detect ART indirectly through the detection of p-AP. Under the optimal conditions, we evaluated the capability of the GQDs based analysis system for the quantitative detection of ART. Fig. 4 displayed the fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs in the presence of different concentrations of ART. As the concentration of ART increased from 0.1 to 55 μM, the fluorescence intensities of GQDs continually decreased while the (F 0-F)/F 0 increased systematically. As show in Fig. 4B, there was a good linear relationship between the (F 0-F)/F 0 and the ART concentration in the range of 0.1–5 μM and 5–55 μM, respectively. The regression equation for the calibration curve can be expressed as (F 0-F)/F 0 = 0.08799 + 0.06351 C ART (μM) and (F 0-F)/F 0 = 0.3905 + 0.00271 C ART (μM) with a correlation coefficient of 0.9994 and 0.9986, respectively. The detection limit was estimated to be 33 nM based on 3Sb/slope, where Sb is the standard deviation of the blank samples. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was 3.65% and 4.12% for the determination of 3 μM and 30 μM ART, respectively.

Comparison between different methods for the determination of ART were presented in Table 1. The results suggested that our developed protocol exhibited higher sensitivity than most of the others. Although it showed comparable sensitivity for the determination of ART [13], pyronin B, as a kind of organic dye, suffered from photobleaching and poor stability in the ambient environment. In contrast, GQDs possess some excellent properties such as low toxicity, stable photoluminescence, good biocompatibility and solubility, which make them a great potential in pharmaceutical analysis.

High specificity for the target analyte is necessary for a new designed IFE sensing assay with potential applications in practical samples. To verify the specificity of the proposed sensing system, the (F 0-F)/F 0 as induced by several potential interfering compounds was also studied under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 5, the (F 0-F)/F 0 increased obviously upon the addition of ART. By contrast, no obvious changes were found by introducing other common coexisting substances including Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, K+, PO4 3-, starch, lactose, dextrin, and magnesium stearate, indicating the good selectivity of the proposed strategy for the determination of ART. The good selectivity of ART was due to the strong oxidability of endoperoxide bridge in ART, which could oxide p-ABA to p-AP, a catalytic substrate of TYR. Therefore, this method can be applied to detect ART in pharmaceutical samples without significant interference.

To evaluate its applicability, the described method was applied to detect the concentrations of ART in pharmaceutical sample. The recoveries of this method were determined by spiking a series of different concentrations of ART into the compound naphthoquine phosphate tablet. The ART content of compound naphthoquine phosphate tablet sample was found to be 7.0 μM. The measured ART concentrations were well matching the corresponding actual ART content (7.5 μM). Three known amounts of standard ART were added into the real samples and the recoveries were tested. The analytical results were shown in Table 2. As a result, the recoveries of 91.7, 93.2, 104.6 for compound naphthoquine phosphate tablets were obtained when 3.0 μM, 13.0 μM, and 35.0 μM of ART were added. Thus, this method is promising in accurate measurement of ART in real samples.

In summary, we have developed a highly selective and sensitive fluorescent method for the determination of ART by using GQDs as the optical reporters. The selective oxidation of p-ABA and ART generated p-AP, which was further oxidized into 4-amino-1,2-benzoquinone by TYR. The presence of 4-amino-1,2-benzoquinone effectively quenched the fluorescence of GQDs due to the IFE. The fluorescence intensities decreased linearly with the increasing concentrations of ART. The detection limit of ART can reach as low as 33 nM. The practical application feasibility of this fluorescent analysis strategy was demonstrated for the determination of ART in tablet. More importantly, the GQDs were used directly without any modification or functionalization. This method can be potentially extended to detect other endoperoxides and many ART derivatives. This work may expand the variety applications of GQDs in pharmaceutical analysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is kindly supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21405094, 21775088, and 81403051), the Natural Science Foundation of Qinghai Province of China (2016-ZJ-955), and the Development Project of Qinghai Key Laboratory (No. 2017-ZJ-Y10), and the Open Funds of Shandong Key Laboratory of TCM Quality Control Technology of Shandong Academy of Sciences.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2019.03.058.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The rise and fall of malaria in a west African rural community, Dielmo, Senegal, from 1990 to 2012: a 22 year longitudinal study

Sensitive and selective electrochemical detection of artemisinin based on its reaction with p-aminophenylboronic acid

Electrochemical determination of artemisinin using a multi-wall carbon nanotube film-modified electrode
Fluorescence-based artemisinin sensing using a pyronin B-doped cellulose film reconstituted from ionic liquid
An enzyme cascade-triggered fluorogenic and chromogenic reaction applied in enzyme activity assay and immunoassay
Turn-on fluorescence sensor based on single-walled-carbon-nanohorn-peptide complex for the detection of thrombin
Fluorescence detection of glutathione reductase activity based on deoxyribonucleic acid-templated silver nanoclusters
Inositol directed facile “green” synthesis of fluorescent gold nanoclusters as selective and sensitive detecting probes of ferric ions
Novel turn-on fluorescent detection of alkaline phosphatase based on green synthesized carbon dots and MnO2 nanosheets
Graphene-quantum-dot assembled nanotubes: a new platform for efficient Raman enhancement
Fluorimetric evaluation of glutathione reductase activity and its inhibitors using carbon quantum dots
A novel label-free fluorescent sensor for highly sensitive detection of bleomycin based on nitrogen-doped grapheme quantum dots
Niche nanoparticle-based FRET assay for bleomycin detection via DNA scission
A ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor for the detection of silver ions using graphene quantum dots
Biosensing platform for the detection of uric acid based on grapheme quantum dots and G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme
A turn-on fluorescent probe for sensitive detection of sulfide anions and ascorbic acid by using sulfanilic acid and glutathione functionalized grapheme quantum dots
A “turn-on” fluorescence sensor for ascorbic acid based on graphene quantum dots via fluorescence resonance energy transfer

Label-free and ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor based on amino-functionalized grapheme quantum dots coupling catalytic G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme for ultrasensitive recognition of human telomere DNA
A novel fluorescence biosensor for sensitivity detection of tyrosinase and acid phosphatase based on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
Probing glutathione reductase activity with grapheme quantum dots and gold nanoparticles system
A fluorometric assay for tyrosinase activity and its inhibitor screening based on graphene quantum dots
Graphene oxide quantumdots@silver core–shell nanocrystals as turn-on fluorescent nanoprobe for ultrasensitive detection of prostate specific antigen
Carbon dots-based fluorescent probes for sensitive and selective detection of iodide
Graphene quantum dots-based fluorescent probe for turn-on sensing of ascorbic acid
Amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots: origin of tunable heterogeneous photoluminescence
Beyond labels: a review of the application of quantum dots as integrated components of assays, bioprobes, and biosensors utilizing optical transduction
Non-redox modulated fluorescence strategy for sensitive and selective ascorbic acid detection with highly photoluminescent nitrogen-doped carbon nanoparticles via solid-state synthesis
Inner filter effect-based sensor for horseradish peroxidase and its application to fluorescence immunoassay
Tuning the aggregation/disaggregation behavior of graphene quantum dots by structure-switching aptamer for high-sensitivity fluorescent ochratoxin A sensor
Electrocatalytic and analytical response of cobalt phthalocyanine modified carbon paste electrodes towards antimalarial endoperoxide artemisinin
Sensitive methods for the spectrophotometric determinations of some antimalarial drugs
The rapid determination of artemisinin by post-column derivatization high-performance liquid chromatography using matrix solid-phase dispersion method
Development of sensitive direct chemiluminescent enzyme immunoassay for the determination of dihydroartemisinin in plasma
